mappings.rationaleAI: The Silk Road's maritime routes directly connected Southeast Asia to China and India, making it central to path A's treatment of South and East Asian influences on traditional Southeast Asian civilizations. The maritime Silk Road also underpins C.2's account of Srivijaya's importance as a node in China trade and B.5.a's Indianized Malayan states.

Thailand's history from Tai confederacies and Mon kingdoms through Sukhothai and Ayutthaya directly covers B.2.b (Sukhothai, c. 1220) and B.2.c (Ayutthaya state, 1350, administrative systems), which are core to the Tai kingdom outline. The article's coverage of Theravada Buddhism and the Tai/Mon relationship also supports B.2 broadly.

Post-classical history (c. 500–1500 CE) provides the broad civilizational and trade-network context—Buddhist and Hindu diffusion, Silk Road linkages—that frames path A's treatment of South and East Asian influences on Southeast Asian civilizations, and its temporal scope encompasses the full span of section B's mainland kingdoms.

Explicitly covers South and Southeast Asian kingdoms including the Khmer, Vietnam, and maritime Southeast Asia, directly addressing path A's historiographic and civilizational overview for the region. Provides the broader Asian context for all of B and C.

Genghis Khan's Mongol campaigns led directly to the destruction of Pagan in 1287, which is specifically described in B.1.c. The article covers the military capacity and strategy that enabled this destruction.

The Mongol Empire's campaigns in mainland Southeast Asia resulted in the destruction of Pagan (B.1.c) and pressured the Khmer state during Jayavarman VII's era and after 1220 (B.3.d–e). The article covers these military incursions and their political consequences for the region.

Mahayana Buddhism and its Vajrayana subset are directly relevant to the Indonesian Archipelago sections: Srivijaya was a major Buddhist centre (C.2), and the Shailendra dynasty of central Java (C.3) and subsequent eastern Javanese kingdoms (C.4) were shaped by Mahayana and tantric Buddhist traditions.

Myanmar directly covers the full arc of B.1—Anyathian culture, Pyu state, the Pagan kingdom, and the Taungoo reunification—spanning the prehistoric period through c. 1600. It is the primary country-level article for this outline path.

The Age of Discovery encompasses the Portuguese conquest of Malacca in 1511, which is the defining event of B.5.b, and early European intrusion into Southeast Asia more broadly. It also contextualizes path A's historiographic framing of the pre-colonial period.

The Spanish Empire controlled the Philippines (referenced in the broader Southeast Asian context) and participated in early European activity in maritime Southeast Asia after the Portuguese conquest of Malacca; relevant at a moderate level to B.5.b's treatment of early European intrusions.

Malaysia's history includes the Malay sultanates, the rise of Malacca as an Islamized sultanate (B.5.a), and the Portuguese conquest of Malacca in 1511 with subsequent 16th-century developments (B.5.b), making it directly relevant to both paths.

Singapore's origins as the maritime emporium Temasek connect to the broader Malayan trading-port tradition and the Indianized states that gave rise to the Malacca sultanate, directly relevant to B.5.a's account of Indianized states and the rise of Malacca.

Jakarta (as Batavia, formerly Sunda Kelapa from the Sunda Kingdom port) ties to the Javanese kingdoms of eastern Java (C.4) and the Majapahit empire era (C.5), providing geographic and historical grounding for the Indonesian Archipelago section.

The early modern period's framing of European colonial onset (c. 1500) directly encompasses the Portuguese conquest of Malacca (B.5.b) and the broader historiographic context for section A's treatment of the transition from traditional to colonially-influenced Southeast Asian civilizations.

At a moderate level, Imperialism's treatment of the earliest wave of European expansion contextualizes the Portuguese conquest of Malacca (1511) that defines B.5.b, providing the theoretical and historical framing for early European intrusion into Southeast Asia.

Ancient history's explicit coverage of Southeast Asia and Austronesia (within its section on South and Southeast Asia) supports path A's broad civilizational overview and paths B.3.a and B.4.a's accounts of prehistoric and early historic peoples in Cambodia and Vietnam.
